---
title: "Regularity Assumption Diagnostics"
author: "David Malison"
date: last-modified
language:
  title-block-published: "Last modified"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    link-external-newwindow: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
          table td { vertical-align: middle; }
          h1.title { font-size: 2.75rem; text-align: center; }
          .quarto-title-meta {
            display: flex !important;
            justify-content: center;
            gap: 5rem;
            text-align: center;
          }
          .assumption {
            margin: 0.75em 0 0.75em 1.5em;
            padding: 0.25em 0.75em;
            border-left: 3px solid #ccc;
          }
        </style>
crossref:
  ref-hyperlink: true
---

In our note on treatment group means, we showed how to perform robust inference under two regularity assumptions.
In this note, we construct diagnostics for whether these assumptions are reasonable in a particular finite sample.

# Variance share diagnostic

We adopt the notation from the treatment group means note.
For $d\in\{0,1\}$, define
$$
\kappa_{d,n}
=
\frac{1}{n} \frac{\max_{1\leq i\leq n}u_{i,d,n}^2}{\,S_{d,n}^2}
$$
and let
$$
\kappa_n = \max\{\kappa_{0,n},\kappa_{1,n}\}.
$$

$\kappa_{n}$ is the largest share of within-arm variation accounted for by a single unit.
[Assumption 3(i)](../treatment-group-means/#asm-nondegenerate) is equivalent to

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{\theta \in \Theta}\kappa_{n} =0
$$

Intuitively, this condition rules out any single unit dominating either potential-outcome sequence.
If $\kappa_{n}$ is large, the variance estimator may be unstable and the CLT approximation unreliable.

It is not possible to compute $\kappa_{n}$ directly because only one potential outcome is observed for each unit.
A feasible alternative is to use the corresponding sample analogue. For $d\in\{0,1\}$, define
$$
\widehat\kappa_{d,n}
=
\frac{1}{n_d}\frac{\max_{1\leq i\leq n}
|Y_i-\widehat{y}_{d,n}|^2 D_{i,d}}{\widehat{S}_{d,n}^2},
$$ {#eq-dominant-unit-diagnostic}
where
$$
n_d=n\overline{D}_{d,n}
$$
denotes the number of units assigned to treatment $d$. Let
$$
\widehat\kappa_n
=
\max\{\widehat\kappa_{0,n},\widehat\kappa_{1,n}\}.
$$
A warning can be triggered whenever $\widehat\kappa_n$ exceeds a maximum variance share tolerance $\overline\kappa$.

# Berry--Esseen diagnostic

Define

$$
\lambda_n
=
\frac{1}{n^{3/2}}\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n |\mathbf{b}_n'\mathbf{u}_{i,n}|^3}{(\mathbf{b}_n'\mathbf{V}_n\mathbf{b}_n)^{3/2}}.
$$ {#eq-lambda-def}

The Berry--Esseen condition in [Assumption 3(ii)](../treatment-group-means/#asm-nondegenerate) is equivalent to

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{\theta\in\Theta}
\lambda_n
=0.
$$

In the treatment group means note, we showed that the Kolmogorov distance between the linearized statistic and the normal distribution is bounded by a constant multiple of $\lambda_n$.
Therefore if this quantity is large, the normal approximation used by the confidence interval may be unreliable.

Since $\mathbf{b}_n'\mathbf{u}_{i,n}$ is unobserved, we cannot compute $\lambda_n$ from the observed data in general.
But if we impose

$$
y_{i,1}=y_{i,0}=y_i
\qquad
\text{for every } i,
$$ {#eq-strong-null}

then $\mathbf{b}_n'\mathbf{u}_{i,n}$ is proportional to $y_i-\overline y$ and (-@eq-lambda-def) reduces to

$$
\lambda_n
=
\frac{1}{n^{3/2}}\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n |y_i-\overline y|^3}{S_n^3},
$$ {#eq-lambda-strong-null}

where

$$
\overline y=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n y_i,
\qquad
S_n^2=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\overline y)^2.
$$

(-@eq-strong-null) is the sharp null assumption used to construct exact p-values in Fisher randomization inference (see [Imbens and Rubin, 2015, Chapter 5](https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/causal-inference-for-statistics-social-and-biomedical-sciences/fishers-exact-pvalues-for-completely-randomized-experiments/23AF990D2EF9C90D0A424D555FACE578)).
Since $Y_i = y_i$ under (-@eq-strong-null), we can compute the feasible diagnostic

$$
\widehat\lambda_n
=
\frac{1}{n^{3/2}}\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n |Y_i-\overline Y|^3}{\widehat S_n^3},
$$ {#eq-lambda-diagnostic-feasible}

where

$$
\overline Y=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i,
\qquad
\widehat S_n^2=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i-\overline Y)^2.
$$

A warning can be triggered whenever $\widehat\lambda_n$ exceeds a maximum tolerance $\overline\lambda$. The tolerance can be chosen such that the Berry--Esseen bound is below a desired threshold. For example, if we want the Kolmogorov distance to be less than $0.05$, we can set

$$
\overline\lambda
=
\frac{0.05}{C}
\frac{\sqrt{p(1-p)}}{p^2+(1-p)^2}
$$

where $C$ is the constant in the Berry--Esseen bound. [Shevtsova (2013)](https://www.mathnet.ru/eng/ia252) gives the upper bound $C\leq0.5583$.

# Simulation

To illustrate how the diagnostic behaves in finite samples, we examine the behavior of the average treatment effect estimator under two different potential outcomes.
In both cases, we simulate under the strong null and set $n=1{,}000$ and $p=0.5$.

## Well-behaved potential outcomes

The first population consists of $1{,}000$ draws from a log-normal distribution with $\sigma=0.3$.
@fig-log-normal-population displays a histogram of the potential outcomes:

![Potential outcomes in the log-normal case.](log_normal_potential_outcomes.png){#fig-log-normal-population}

@fig-log-normal-sampling-distribution shows that the sampling distribution of $\sqrt{n}(\widehat\psi_n-\psi_n)/\widehat\sigma_n$ closely tracks $N(0,1)$, even though the potential outcomes are skewed.

![Sampling distribution of $\sqrt{n}(\widehat\psi_n-\psi_n)/\widehat\sigma_n$ in the log-normal case.](log_normal_sampling_distribution.png){#fig-log-normal-sampling-distribution}


## Potential outcomes with outliers

The first population consists of $997$ standard-normal draws plus $3$ extreme outliers at $y=60$.
This finite population produces $\widehat\varepsilon_n=0.286$.
@fig-be-failing-population displays a histogram of the observations:

![Potential outcomes in the outlier case.](outlier_potential_outcomes.png){#fig-be-failing-population}

@fig-be-failing shows that the sampling distribution of $\sqrt{n}(\widehat\psi_n-\psi_n)/\widehat\sigma_n$ is multimodal.
The modes correspond to the number of outliers assigned to treatment.

![Sampling distribution of $\sqrt{n}(\widehat\psi_n-\psi_n)/\widehat\sigma_n$ in the outlier case.](outlier_sampling_distribution.png){#fig-be-failing}
